subject: Urology
title: Testicular Torsion: The 6-Hour Clock
hook: A teenager with sudden scrotal pain is a clock you cannot stop. Move, or lose the testis.

points.heading: Why it twists
points.body: Torsion twists the spermatic cord, first obstructing venous outflow. Oedema builds, then arterial inflow fails, ending in necrosis. The classic predisposer is the bell clapper deformity, a high insertion of the tunica vaginalis.

points.heading: A useful but imperfect sign
points.body: The cremasteric reflex is usually absent on the affected side, a helpful pointer but not reliable on its own. A present reflex does not exclude torsion. Pair it with sudden severe pain, nausea and vomiting, and never let it delay exploration.

points.heading: Doppler confirms, never delays
points.body: Doppler shows absent central testicular flow. If Doppler is unavailable or the picture is doubtful, explore surgically rather than wait. Imaging must never cost you the testis.

points.heading: Fix both sides
points.body: Treatment is exploration, detorsion and orchidopexy of the affected testis. Always fix the contralateral testis too, because the same anatomy predisposes it to torsion.

caption: Testicular torsion is the question examiners love because the answer is time. Salvage is near 100% within 6 hours and falls sharply beyond 24 hours, so sudden pain with a high-riding testis means theatre, not delay. Recall Engine turns any lecture into SBAs, flashcards and high-yield notes so the 6-hour rule sticks before exam day. Start free, link in bio.
